learning_outcomes.description: Understand in-depth contemporary issues and practices in journalism and intercultural communication.
Critically examine varying systems of journalism and/or communication practices.
Report on a community in a nuanced way.
Utilise a variety of research tools, including scholarly journals, interviews and the Internet.
Build and maintain a news product and platform.
Reflect critically on journalism as industry and its role as a community change agent.

assessments.task_description: You will be marked on your capacity to work as part of a team from the field. You will also be marked on your ability to produce and deliver stories in the field.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description: You will be graded on your body of work. There is both a quantity and quality and element to this. As you move through the early part of the course you will develop story ideas that you execute in the field. Part of your grade will be based on your ability to execute a story that is suited to the tastes, interests and values of your audience. You are required to produce at least three stories across different platforms and mediums. One must comply to traditional news style while the other two can apply other narrative styles.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: You are required to write a proposal to a key player in the journalism industry detailing why they need to be investing and engaging in community journalism in Queensland. You are required to draw from a variety of industry and academic sources in making your argument. You might also draw upon your experience doing journalism in the community.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard Assignments. Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Trauma and ethical reporting practice

This week we return to questions of what it means to do "good" journalism. Beyond the usual concerns of fairness, balance and accuracy, this means delving into protecting your sources and taking care of yourself. Psychologist and former Director of the Dart Center for Journalism and Trauma, Dr Cait McMahon, will be joining us.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7: Trauma and ethical reporting practice This week we return to questions of what it means to do "good" journalism.
